Date: 12/11/2023

# Abstract

This project addresses the communication challenges faced by the deaf and hard-of-hearing community by leveraging artificial intelligence. The central focus is on empowering disabled individuals through the development of an AI-driven sign language communication system. The research acknowledges the critical importance of inclusive communication for people with disabilities, particularly those who use sign language. The study explores the complexities of sign language communication and the existing limitations in access to interpreters. The initial research culminates in creating a system interface that allows sign language users to use a camera to capture hand movements and then convert the imagery to text. Users simultaneously have access to a sign keyboard, enabling them to effectively communicate with sign language users by adopting a visual and symbol-based method. The system uses American sign language signs recorded with a camera; Mediapipe was used to extract key points from the face, pose, and hands, and a Transformer and random classifier were used to train our model in predicting real-time signs. The overall performance of the model was at an accuracy of 0.9. The project's significance lies in empowering disabled individuals, fostering inclusivity in society, and contributing to technological advancements in AI for social impact.  
Keywords: *Machine learning, American Sign Language, Accuracy, Long Short-Term Memory.*

# **Chapter One: Proposal** {#chapter-one:-proposal}

## 1.1 	Introduction  {#1.1-introduction}

Empowering people with disabilities has become crucial in creating a more inclusive and equal society. 15% of the world's population live with a disability, and the prevalence of disabilities is higher in developing nations\[1\]. Compared to persons without disabilities, people with disabilities typically have worse health, achieve worse in school, have fewer job possibilities, and have more excellent rates of poverty. This is primarily because of the need for more resources and the numerous challenges they deal with daily\[2\]. Nevertheless, there are still wide discrepancies in communication access due to the absence of sign language interpreters, a lack of knowledge of sign language, and the restricted accessibility to sign language resources\[3\]. The communication gap that frequently isolates the Deaf and hard-of-hearing community is a significant concern. Just 41 countries worldwide recognize sign language as an official language, of which 21 are in the European Union \[4\]. This limits the availability of sign language teachers/interpreters, leading to the exclusion of deaf people from most schools\[18\]. This project aims to develop a system that enables real-time sign language interpretation, enhances the accuracy of sign language recognition, and delivers accessible sign language content by utilizing AI.

## 1.2      Problem Statement {#1.2-problem-statement}

The deaf and hard-of-hearing community encounters substantial communication challenges, intensified by a shortage of efficient sign language communication methods, particularly during the COVID-19 pandemic because it is centered on human interpretation. According to WHO, nearly 80% of people with disabling hearing loss live in low and middle-income countries\[1\]. To ensure full societal inclusion and the right to effective communication for people with disabilities, there is a need to develop machine-based solutions for real-time, precise, and sign language interpretation.

## 1.3     Primary Objective {#1.3-primary-objective}

This project aims to develop a web-based interface that performs real-time detection of sign language, translates it to text and allows users to use a sign language keyboard to allow two-way communication.

##  1.4     Specific objectives {# 1.4- -specific-objectives}

- To discover and evaluate existing sign language recognition systems, including existing AI-based systems used worldwide.  
-  To identify and consolidate the gaps in the existing systems.   
- To Evaluate the different machine learning models and design a solution based on the identified gaps for implementation.

## 1.5     Research Questions {#1.5 -research-questions}

- How can real-time sign language interpretation for deaf and hard-of-hearing people be efficiently facilitated by artificial intelligence technologies?   
- What are the key challenges and barriers in developing and implementing AI-driven sign language communication solutions, and how can these obstacles be mitigated?  
- What is the optimal model for developing an AI-based sign language interpretation system?

## 1.6    Technical Requirements {#1.6 - -technical-requirements}

- Computing Infrastructure: The project will require a computer with an i7 processor to build the model.  
- Data Analysis Software: The project will use Google Collab to analyze the data, plus libraries like Pandas and Numpy for data analysis.  
- Research Datasets: The project will use the Google isolated sign language dataset.  
- Deployment: The project will use Streamlit for local and cloud deployment.

## 1.7    Proposal Significance {#1.7 - -proposal-significance}

This study has profound implications for individuals with disabilities, particularly those using sign language. Leveraging artificial intelligence, the project aims to enhance access to healthcare, work, education, and social interaction, fostering independence and improving overall quality of life. Beyond individual empowerment, the study aligns with the broader goal of building an inclusive society by providing equal opportunities for everyone, regardless of communication preferences. Moreover, this technological advancement in AI addresses immediate social challenges. It is a pioneering example of creative AI solutions in various fields, reflecting a broader impact on technological innovation and societal progress.

## 1.8    Scope  {#1.8- -scope}

- The project will use American sign language.  
- The system will be web-based using Streamlit.  
- The data will come from Kaggle.  
  


## 1.9    Limitations {#1.9 - -limitations}

- Cultural Variations: Addressing the cultural and linguistic diversity within sign language communities can be complex and requires extensive research and collaboration to ensure cultural sensitivity in AI systems.  
- Research Timeframe: Time limitations may restrict the depth and breadth of the project.

# **Chapter Two: Literature Review** {#chapter-two:-literature-review}

## 2.1	   Introduction To Sign Language {#2.1-introduction-to-sign-language}

Sign language is a communication mode involving hand gestures mainly used by the speech and hearing-impaired community\[7\]. According to the World Health Organization, 1 in 5 people worldwide live with hearing loss, and most do not have access to interventions\[6\]. Nearly 80% of people with disabling hearing loss live in low- and middle-income countries\[8\]. The United Nations Convention on the Rights of Persons with Disabilities article 23.3 requires the governments to provide early and comprehensive information, services, and support to children with disabilities and their families, including information about deaf culture, sign language, and bilingual education\[9\]. However, Information about deaf culture is hardly accessible online, and worldwide, just 1- 2% of deaf individuals receive their education via sign language\[9\]. Strides have been made in integrating sign language into the general school curriculum, but the adoption rate still needs to be higher. For example, in Australia, After extensive lobbying by the deaf community, the first Australian national curriculum for Australian Sign Language (Auslan) was introduced in December 2016\. Despite this, only 4% of Victorian public schools teach Auslan\[10\]. 

## 2.2* *    Sign Language Detection Techniques*   * {#figure-1-a-diagram-showing-an-overview-of-the-system  }

There are two approaches to sign language recognition techniques. These techniques are based on the gadget used\[11\]. The two approaches include the vision-based approach and the data glove approach. The vision-based approach uses a camera to capture images or videos, which will be used in image processing\[11\]. The image is compared against the database of images. The vision-based approach has a lower computational cost\[11\]. This approach is resolution dependent, i.e., the ability of your camera to capture high-resolution images. The data gloves approach is one where gloves with sensors capture the pose of the hands and motions\[11\]. According to Nimisha and Jacob, this approach highly depends on the sensors. This method is highly accurate but costly. This project will focus on the vision-based approach since the glove-based would require users to wear gloves physically. 

![][image2]

*Figure 2 The basic overview of a vision-based technique* 

An essential aspect of implementing image processing is differentiating objects using color or black and white. One thing to note, according to Pramada, who proposed a system that turns sign to text to speech, is that using black and white reduces the amount of data to analyze and makes it hard to differentiate skin and markers from the background. After image acquisition, the captured image is turned into a binary image during processing. A binary image is an image that has only two possible values for each pixel \[12\].

## 2.3.    Analysis Of the Existing Sign Detection System {#2.3.- analysis-of-the-existing-sign-detection-system}

Halder and Tayade demonstrate a system for sign language interpretation against multiple machine learning and deep learning algorithms. Their approach recognizes the need for compelling image preprocessing to make a highly accurate model. Therefore, they used an open-source framework from Google, "Media pipe," which has a high detection capability for human body parts. In their approach, they used the following machine-learning model: Artificial Neural Network (ANN), Random Forest, Decision tree, Naive Bayes, multi-layer perceptron (M.L.P.), support vector machine (SVM), and K-nearest neighbor \[13\].

The following are the results of their work:

![][image3]  
*Table 1 Accuracy obtained using machine learning and deep learning algorithms.*

In conclusion, The SVM algorithm performed better than the other algorithms \[13\]. Anup et al. proposed a method for detecting sign language and turning speech into signs. This approach also uses the SVM algorithm for classification. The model performed with an accuracy of 93% on gesture recognition\[15\]. Speech-to-sign accuracy varies depending on factors like background noise\[15\]. Xia et al. proposed a solution for medical consultation."Heart-speaker" is a solution that enables doctors to interpret for patients by analyzing the patient's signs. It is also equipped with speech recognition that allows the translation of voice information to sign video. In their approach, they used the deep learning algorithm Mobile Net network. The algorithm is based on CNN and is used for real-time and fast detection. Their report does acknowledge that a noisier environment can lead to a worse recognition effect\[14\]. The achieved accuracy was 90%. The authors also recognize that their accuracy is lower than a system using a media pipe with the R.N.N. model but emphasize that the system is faster in recognition\[14\]. 

## 2.4     Significance of the Project {#2.4- - -significance-of-the-project}

The existing projects focus on using speech recognition to translate into sign language. While this approach is very innovative, it limits the system because different factors determine the accuracy of speech recognition systems. Some of the challenges in speech recognition include background noise and the speaker's variation in articulation due to factors like stress, differences in pronunciation, and stress on words \[16\]. The different challenges could lead to word mismatch. The proposed solution is a symbol-based approach that takes text to find a corresponding sign. This would reduce the risk of having a word mismatch in the system to maintain the solution's efficacy, performance, and adoption. Finally, Existing systems are not hosted online. The proposed system will be hosted on the cloud to facilitate access to the system.

## 2.5     Conclusion {#2.5 - -conclusion}

Gaps To Bridge In The Development Of Sign Development Detection

After analyzing the literature, the following will be incorporated to develop the sign language detection system.

- The existing system turns the signs into speech and then speech into signs, which reduces accuracy. This system will detect signs and turn them into text; vice versa, it will turn text into signs.  
- The system will be available online, unlike existing systems.


# **Chapter Three: Requirements Gathering, Analysis, And Methodology** {#chapter-three:-requirements-gathering,-analysis,-and-methodology}

## 3.1      Summary Of  Software Requirements Specification {#3.1-summary-of-software-requirements-specification}

Two machine learning algorithms, LSTM and SVM, will be compared to find the best-performing model during training. The users will access their system camera to capture their hand movements and face position. Using Mediapipe and OpenCV, the system will map out the coordinates of the hands and faces, which will be passed to the trained model for prediction. The prediction will be the equivalence of the sign in text. The users will also have access to a sign keyboard for communication with people who do not know sign language.

## 3.2      Conceptual Framework {#3.2-conceptual-framework}

The suggested approach comprises creating a system interface allowing sign language users to use a camera to capture hand movements. The system will be coupled to a deep learning model to translate sign language gestures into text displayed on the interface. Users will simultaneously have access to a sign language keyboard, enabling them to effectively communicate with sign language users by adopting a visual and symbol-based method.

![][image4]  
*Figure 1 A diagram showing an overview of the system  *

## 3.3      Methodology {#3.3-methodology}

I will use Iterative development to break down an extensive application's software development life cycle (SDLC) into smaller chunks for this project. It is typically used with incremental development in which a longer SDLC is split into smaller segments that build upon each other\[17\].

![][image5]

I will use data from the Kaggle dataset collected by [Google](https://www.kaggle.com/competitions/asl-signs/data) for the training.

## 3.4      Functional Requirements  {#3.4-functional-requirements}

- Camera caption:  The system must allow users to capture sign language gestures through a camera.  
- Interpretation: The system must use a machine learning model to interpret the sign and produce text, and on the other hand, the text should produce relevant signs.  
- Sign Keyboard Interface: Users can access a keyboard with different sign language signs.  
- User interface: Users will have an easy-to-use chat-like interface that does not require technical expertise.  
  


## 3.5      Non-functional Requirements {#3.5-non-functional-requirements}

- Performance: The system should process sign language accurately and in real time with minimal latency.  
- Reliability: The system should have an uptime of at least 99.9%.  
- Scalability: The system should be scalable to accommodate growing users.  
- Usability: The system interface should be intuitive for users with varying technical expertise.  
- Availability: The system should be available 24/7, except during scheduled maintenance.  
- Security: The system should comply with industry standards for data encryption.

## 3.6      Standard Development Tools Used  {#3.6-standard-development-tools-used}

Computing 

- Operating System: Windows Operating System, Intel Core i7   
- Ram: 16GB

Software Tools

- Tensorflow: To provide us with the deep learning algorithm, in this case, Conv1D and transformers.   
- Scikit-Learn: To provide access to random forest classifier algorithm and data preprocessing  
- Google Colaboratory Notebooks: Environment for training our model.  
- Visual studio code: The development environment for developing the website for sign detection.  
- Chrome:  Needed during the development and debugging of the website  
- Streamlit: Host our website as well as our database

## 3.7      Programming Languages & Frameworks {#3.7-programming-languages-&-frameworks}

- Python: For model development, training, and testing.  
- Streamlit: Framework for building and deploying the model to a web application.




# **Chapter Four: System Design** {#chapter-four:-system-design}

## 4.1     Introduction {#4.1-introduction}

This chapter explains how the system for real-time prediction was implemented. It will dive into how various functionalities were implemented and integrated and finally showcase how the system will function.

## 4.2    Data Exploration {#4.2-data-exploration}

The dataset used for the training model was collected from Kaggle and . ![][image6]

*Figure 3 Sample of dataset*

Each sequence ID has a corresponding file with a dataset containing the x,y, and z coordinates of the hands for each phrase.

![][image7]

*Figure 4 Sample of dataset*

Since the data was too big, I used TFRecords to facilitate model building and training. TFRecords (TensorFlow Records) are a binary file format used in TensorFlow to store data efficiently\[19\]. They are commonly used to store large datasets easily for reading and processing by TensorFlow during training or inference\[19\]. TFRecords are especially useful when dealing with large-scale datasets such as images, audio samples, or text data. The data was also collected from [Kaggle.](https://www.kaggle.com/code/shlomoron/aslfr-parquets-to-tfrecords-cleaned)

## 4.3  Data preparation and preprocessing {#4.3-data-preparation-and-preprocessing}

- Since we are dealing with sequence input generation, the data preprocessing involves defining tokens to manage the sequences during preprocessing.

![][image8]

*Figure 5 Creating tokens for sequence preprocessing*

The start token indicates the beginning of a sequence, and the end token indicates its end. The pad token is used to pad sequences to a fixed length during batch processing.

- The following steps included extracting the x,y, and z coordinates to create a frame that is resized to create a data frame that can be fed into the model. The frames are later matched to their respective representation of character.  
- Finally, the TFRecord is split into training and testing.

![][image9]

*Figure 6 Splitting the data into train and test* 

## 4.4   Model Development and Optimization {#4.4-model-development-and-optimization}

As mentioned earlier, we are dealing with sequence generation, and the Transformer suits the task better. Transformers are designed to process sequential input data\[20\]. It was defined with the Keras 1D convolution layer to train our model. The dropout technique was used to optimize the model by regularizing the model. The transformer model design is similar to the one used in the Automatic Speech Recognition with Transformer tutorial for Keras\[21\]. 

![][image10]

*Figure 7 model summary* 

## 4.5   Model fitting  {#4.5-model-fitting}

The hyper-parameters determined for my transformer model is as follows 

- Validation split \= 0.3  
- epochs \=13  
- batch\_size=32  
- Verbose \=2

## 4.6 Post-training of the model {#4.6-post-training-of-the-model}

After the model was developed, there was a need to train it to extract the coordinates from images, relate them to the sign, and perform classification.

I used the following dataset of images from [Kaggle](https://www.kaggle.com/datasets/danrasband/asl-alphabet-test) for alphabets and [this dataset](https://www.kaggle.com/datasets/rayeed045/american-sign-language-digit-dataset) for numbers to train my model to extract the coordinates of the hands. I used a random forest classifier to classify the sign with coordinates. This was done to enable real-time detection of the signs.

# Chapter Five: Evaluation and Results {#chapter-five:-evaluation-and-results}

## 5.1      Introduction {#5.1-introduction}

This chapter will present the results obtained from training the system and making predictions on the data. 70% of the dataset was used for training and 30% for testing.

## 

## 5.2     Model testing and evaluation {#5.2-model-testing-and-evaluation}

 Loss vs. val\_loss

- The "loss" typically refers to the training loss, also known as the training error or training objective. The "val\_loss" refers to the validation loss, which measures how well the model generalizes to new, unseen data. The image below shows the model was performing well.

![][image13]  
Figure 10 loss vs val\_loss 

I also evaluated the model's ability to classify sign alphabets using a confusion matrix.

# ![][image14]

*Figure 11 Confusion matrix* 

- Accuracy: 0.9   
  


Sequence generation

Additionally for my model, I used the Levenshtein distance to evaluate my models ability to generate sequences of letters. Levenshtein distance, also known as edit distance, is a metric used to measure the difference between two sequences of characters\[22\]. It calculates the minimum number of single-character edits (insertions, deletions, or substitutions) required to change one sequence into another\[22\]. I calculated the accuracy of the model predictions by comparing the Levenshtein distance.To achieve this I compared the original sequence from testing data with the generated sequence from the model as shown below where the top line represents the actual sequence (referred to as ground truth in my code) and the bottom line represents the generated(referred to as pred-lists).

![][image15]  
Figure 15 Model predictions

The process returned an array of the Levenshtein distance as seen below   
![][image16]  
Figure 16 Levenshtein distance

I then computed the overall average Levenshtein distance ;The closer the Levenshtein distance to zero and the higher the accuracy value, the better the model's performance\[22\].

- Average= 7.5

## 5.3   Model Performance Analysis {#5.3-model-performance-analysis}

The performance of the sign detection model is high when it comes to recognizing individual letters with an accuracy of 0.9. However, when it comes to sequential recognition, i.e., detecting a series of signs, the model performance goes down with a Levenshtein distance of on average 7.5.

##

The first line in the output below shows the actual sequence (word) vs the predicted sequence.

![][image17]

# Chapter Six: Conclusion And Recommendation {#chapter-six:-conclusion-and-recommendation}

## 6.1    Conclusion {#6.1-conclusion}

 The real-time detection system can accurately detect the signs with an accuracy of 0.9 for single alphabet detection. The system also provides access to a keyboard that translates sign-to-text and text-to-sign, and the system was deployed using the Streamlit library. One limitation of the system is that the model performance is still low due to time constraints and needs improvement regarding sequential text generation of sign interpretation. For future plans, the intention is to improve the model even more and integrate more sign languages like Rwandan sign language as data on the language is being collected on the different African sign languages.

## 6.2    Recommendations {#6.2-recommendations}

- Government and public service providers can use the system to facilitate communication with sign language users.  
- Hospitals can use the system to facilitate communication between doctors and patients.  
- Schools can also use the system to teach students sign language.

In conclusion, the project not only aims to bridge the communication gap for the deaf and hard-of-hearing community but also advocates for systemic changes to create an inclusive and supportive environment. Implementing these recommendations will contribute to the empowerment, inclusion, and improved quality of life for individuals with hearing disabilities. 

# Chapter Seven: Bibliography {#chapter-seven:-bibliography}

\[1\]    World Bank. "Disability Inclusion Overview." \[Online\]. Available: https://www.worldbank.org/en/topic/disability. \[Accessed: 12 October 2023\].

\[2\]   K. D. Seelman, "The World Health Organization/World Bank’s first world report on disability," International Journal of Telerehabilitation, pp. 11–14, 2011\. DOI: 10.5195/ijt.2011.6076.

\[3\]   National Deaf Center, "The ASL Interpreter Shortage and Its Impact on Accessibility in College Settings," Jan. 17, 	2023.\[Online\]. Available:https://nationaldeafcenter.org/news-items/the-asl-interpreter-shortage-and-its-impact-on-accessibility-in-college-settings/. \[Accessed: 12 OCTOBER 2023\].

\[4\] "2020 GEM Report: Inclusion and Education," Feb. 18, 2021.\[Online\]. Available: https://gem-report-2020.unesco.org/thematic/. \[Accessed: 11 OCTOBER 2023\].

\[5\]     I. Papastratis, C. Chatzikonstantinou, D. Konstantinidis, K. Dimitropoulos, and P. Daras, "Artificial Intelligence Technologies for Sign Language," MDPI Sensors, vol. 21, no. 17, p. 5843, Aug. 30, 2021\. DOI: 10.3390/s21175843.

\[6\]   World Health Organization (WHO), "World Report on Hearing," Mar. 2, 2021\. \[Online\]. Available: https://www.who.int/multi-media/details/world-report-on-hearing. \[Accessed: 14 SEPTEMBER 2023\].

\[7\]  Canadian Association of the Deaf \- Association des Sourds du Canada, "Terminology," 2022\. \[Online\]. Available: https://cad-asc.ca/issues-positions/terminology/. \[Accessed: 12 SEPTEMBER 2023\].

\[8\] World Health Organization (WHO), "Deafness and Hearing Loss," \[Online\].Available: https://www.who.int/news-room/fact-sheets/detail/deafness-and-hearing-loss. \[Accessed:14 SEPTEMBER 2023\].

\[9\]   World Federation of the Deaf (WFD), "Human Rights of the Deaf," Aug. 11, 2017\. \[Online\].Available: https://wfdeaf.org/our-work/human-rights-of-the-deaf/. \[Accessed:14 SEPTEMBER 2023 \].

\[10\]    H. Bowman-Smart, C. Gyngell, A. Morgan, and J. Savulescu, "The moral case for sign language education," Monash Bioethics Review, Nov. 23, 2019\. \[Online\]. Available: https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s40592-019-00101-0. \[Accessed: 14 SEPTEMBER 2023\].

\[11\]   K. Nimisha and A. Jacob, "A brief review of the recent trends in sign language recognition," in 2020 International Conference on Communication and Signal Processing (ICCSP), 2020\. DOI: 10.1109/iccsp48568.2020.9182351.

\[12\]   S. Pramada, "Intelligent sign language recognition using image processing," IOSR Journal of Engineering, vol. 03, no. 02, pp. 45–51, 2013\. DOI: 10.9790/3021-03224551.

\[13\]   A. Tayade and A. Halder, "Real-time vernacular sign language recognition using MediaPipe and Machine Learning," May   2021\.  \[Online\].  Available: https://www.researchgate.net/profile/Akshit-Tayade-2/publication/369945035\_Realtime\_Vernacular\_Sign\_Language\_Recognition\_using\_MediaPipe\_and\_Machine\_Learning/links/643605da20f25554da283357/Real-time-Vernacular-Sign-Language-Recognition-using-MediaPipe-and-Machine-Learning.pdf. \[Accessed: 14 SEPTEMBER 2023\].

\[14\]   K. Xia, W. Lu, H. Fan, and Q. Zhao, "A sign language recognition system applied to deaf-mute medical consultation," MDPI Sensors, Nov. 24, 2022\. DOI: 10.3390/s22239107.

\[15\]   Anul. K., K. Thankachan, and M. M. Dominic, "Sign language recognition," in 2016 3rd International Conference on Recent Advances in Information Technology (RAIT) \[Preprint\], 2016\. DOI: 10.1109/rait.2016.7507939.

\[16\]   P. Sahu, M. Dua, and A. Kumar, "Challenges and issues in adopting speech recognition," Advances in Intelligent Systems and Computing, pp. 209–215, 2017\. DOI: 10.1007/978-981-10-6626-9\_23.

\[17\]   R. Awati, "What is iterative development?: Definition from TechTarget," Software Quality, June 13, 2023\. \[Online\]. Available: https://www.techtarget.com/searchsoftwarequality/definition/iterative-development. \[Accessed: 10 SEPTEMBER 2023\].

\[18\]    M. Stewart, "Deaf and hard-of-hearing community suffers from a dearth of diverse interpreters," INSIGHT Into 	 Diversity, Nov. 17, 2020\. \[Online\]. Available: https://www.insightintodiversity.com/deaf-and-hard-of-hearing-community-suffers-from-dearth-of-diverse-interpreters/. \[Accessed: 10 SEPTEMBER 2023\].

\[19\]  "TFRecord and tf.train.example," TensorFlow Core. \[Online\]. Available: https://www.tensorflow.org/tutorials/load\_data/tfrecord\#:\~:text=The%20TFRecord%20format%20is%20a,to%20understand%20a%20message%20type. \[Accessed: Mar. 20, 2024\].

\[20\]  "What are transformers? \- Transformers in artificial intelligence," Amazon Web Services. \[Online\]. Available: https://aws.amazon.com/what-is/transformers-in-artificial-intelligence/. \[Accessed: Mar. 20, 2024\].  
\[21\]  K. Team, "Keras Documentation: Automatic speech recognition with Transformer," Keras. \[Online\]. Available: https://keras.io/examples/audio/transformer\_asr/. \[Accessed: Mar. 20, 2024\].

\[22\]  "Levenshtein Distance \- an overview," ScienceDirect Topics. \[Online\]. Available: https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/levenshtein-distance\#:\~:text=The%20Levenshtein%20distance%20(Levenshtein%201966,transform%20one%20string%20into%20another. \[Accessed: Mar. 20, 2024\].
